data.title: Crypto business backed by Reform donor loses £1.5bn on gold rout
data.description:
A crypto business backed by Reform’s biggest donor has suffered a $2bn (£1.5bn) hit from a sell-off in gold.

Tether, which counts billionaire Christopher Harborne as one of its key shareholders, has been hammered by a 20pc drop in the precious metal’s price since January.

While its primary business is crypto, Tether has become the world’s largest corporate buyer of gold in recent years, which it uses alongside traditional currencies and other assets to underwrite the value of its digital token.

Tether had amassed 148 tonnes of gold worth more than $23.3bn as of Jan 31, according to a report by investment bank Jefferies.

This is greater than reserves held by Australia, the UAE or Qatar, as the company increased its holdings over the past year to capitalise on gold hitting record highs.

However, the value of that vast stockpile is now in freefall after the gold price has tumbled in recent months.

Gold is currently valued at $4,444, down from $4,900 at the end of January when Jefferies made their calculations. This 9.3pc drop implies a loss in value of more than $2bn for Tether’s gold holdings, now estimated at around $21bn.

According to Tether boss Paolo Ardoino, the crypto company has been buying up around $1bn in gold every month with the aim of the precious metal accounting for 15pc of its entire investment portfolio.

Tether has been amassing gold in a former nuclear bunker in Switzerland, which Mr Ardoino described as a “James Bond kind of place”.

The company has been hugely profitable thanks to its investments and interest income.

Tether said in December it had $192.8bn in total assets to back up $186.5bn of Tether tokens that it has issued to the market, an excess of $6.3bn. Its holdings generated $10bn in profits for the business last year.

As a so-called stablecoin, Tether’s token is designed to maintain a value of $1. The crypto coins are backed up one-to-one by vast holdings of regular currencies, US treasuries and assets like gold to maintain its value.

But the volatile price of gold in the wake of Donald Trump’s attack on Iran and the accompanying oil price shock has raised doubts over its status as a safe-haven investment.

Despite Tether’s profits, a report by S&P last year assessed its ability to retain its peg to the US dollar was “weak”, citing its exposure to assets including Bitcoin and gold.

In response to the report, Mr Ardoino said last year: “We wear your loathing with pride.” This week, Tether confirmed it would be conducting a full audit of its business. The Financial Times reported it had hired KPMG for the review.

It comes after Mr Harborne, a reclusive businessman based in Thailand, handed Nigel Farage’s Reform a further £3m earlier this month, on top of a £9m donation last year.

Mr Harborne was one of the first investors in Tether. He is believed to have a stake of approximately 12pc in the business behind the digital currency.

Tether was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Water boss accused of potential conflict of interest over lobbying ties
data.description:
The chairman of a water quango that sticks up for consumers has been accused of a potential conflict of interest because of his stake in a lobbying firm that works with utilities.

Rob Wilson, who runs the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), is still listed as a senior adviser at Political Insight UK, The Telegraph can reveal.

Mr Wilson, a former Conservative minister under David Cameron, co-founded the firm and is a minority shareholder.

Founded in 2020, Political Insight UK claims on its website to help clients with “government relations” by advising them on “policy development and regulatory issues”.

This includes clients across a range of sectors, such as transport, housing, retail and public utilities. The website makes no mention of individual water companies, though the term utilities covers the water industry.

His links to the lobbying firm have raised concern owing to his role at CCW, a government-approved quango funded by households and businesses.

The CCW brands itself as the voice of billpayers by holding companies, government and regulators to account.

Alistair Carmichael, chairman of the environment, food and rural affairs committee, said: “Public confidence in the water industry is already at an all-time low.

“A body like the CCW has to be seen to be the consumers’ voice and nothing else. This is not a moment where anyone can be seen to be riding two horses.”

After publication, Mr Wilson claimed that he had not undertaken any client advisory work for the firm for more than two years, and none at all since his appointment as CCW chairman. He said the website was outdated.

He also stressed that before that, he did not advise any clients in the public utilities sector. He denies that his stake in Political Insight gives rise to any conflict of interest regarding his role at the CCW.

The row will heighten pressure on Mr Wilson after a cache of receipts revealed how he twice claimed discounts on train tickets last year by using a railcard for students.

On one occasion, he was caught by a conductor and asked to pay a £16.40 fare correction, which he later expensed.

The CCW said Mr Wilson had clicked the 16-25 railcard by accident and made the mistake in “good faith”.

Mr Wilson previously drew scrutiny during his time as a Tory MP for claiming 9p for a 320-metre car journey.

As well as owning a stake in Political Insight UK, the former minister is also a director for Transport Focus, a consumer action group for rail passengers.

In recent years, Transport Focus has worked in tandem with government officials to combat fare evasion.

The CCW says on its website that Mr Wilson, who was appointed chairman by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) last year, has a “number of shareholdings declared”.

However, the group said that “the list does not include any water companies”.

‘Radical transparency’

A CCW spokesman said the conflict of interest claims were a matter for Defra.

Defra asks public appointees to disclose any potential conflicts of interest to the department and puts mitigation measures in place if necessary.

Speaking last year, Mr Wilson called for “radical transparency” and greater accountability to rebuild trust in the water sector.

He also spoke to water companies about how to address whether their actions are acceptable at a conference.

“If this were explained on the front page of tomorrow’s newspaper, would the public see it as fair, responsible and in their interest?” he said, addressing attendees in his capacity as CCW chairman.

“If the answer is yes, you are rebuilding confidence. If the answer is no, you are eroding it.”

Scrutiny of Mr Wilson’s lobbying ties comes at an awkward time for the CCW.

The group has so far failed to publish financial accounts as it battles with the National Audit Office (NAO) amid a dispute over its expenditure.

Analysis of the CCW’s board minutes from last year also highlights how the group is in a separate spat with HMRC.

A CCW spokesman said this matter concerned a decision not to include VAT on its sales invoices.

“After extensive communication with HMRC, we have now become VAT registered to allow us to correct the issue, which we are in the process of doing,” the spokesman said.

“Having revisited the calculations HMRC provides, we understand any resulting interest and surcharges will be minimal.”

Defra was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Europe facing fuel shortage within days, warns Shell boss
data.description:
Europe risks fuel shortages as soon as next month as the fallout from the Middle East crisis deepens, the boss of Shell has warned.

Wael Sawan, the company’s chief executive, said the global oil and gas supply squeeze had already forced parts of Asia to cut energy consumption and that the “ripple effect” threatens to spread west within days.

He warned this meant European governments may need to curb energy demand – a step not taken since the 2022 energy crisis – to prevent shortages.

Around one fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG) supplies are trapped in the Gulf following Iran’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Military sources said on Tuesday that Britain was leading efforts to reopen the vital trade choke point, with planners considering deploying mine-hunting drones from chartered civilian “motherships”.

Meanwhile, Donald Trump is preparing to send in paratroopers for a possible ground invasion of key islands along the Iranian coast. Yet, the US president also insisted on Tuesday that peace talks with Tehran were taking place.

JD Vance, the vice-president, is being lined up as a negotiator for the first time as Iran is refusing to talk with Mr Trump’s Middle East envoys, sources told The Telegraph.

Gulf sources said that the Iranians would not meet Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, accusing them of “backstabbing” at previous nuclear talks before the war started.

With the conflict now in its fourth week, the International Energy Agency has called on countries to reduce oil and gas consumption by encouraging more remote working, lower road speed limits, and greater use of public transport.

In Asia, where countries are heavily reliant on oil and gas from the Middle East, governments have already imposed four-day working weeks, urged people to use less air conditioning and suspended overseas trips for bureaucrats.

Speaking at an industry conference in Houston, Texas, Mr Sawan said European governments may soon need to take similar measures.

“It is a ripple effect,” he said. “We see south Asia first to get that brunt, that moves to south-east Asia, north-east Asia and then more so into Europe as we get into April.

“So we are trying to work with governments to alert them to the levers they may need to pull – including demand‑side measures, what they need to do around storage, what they need to do around purchasing stock and so on and so forth.”

Bankers have said a prolonged energy shock could plunge Britain into a recession, while the RAC Foundation said rising petrol prices had already cost drivers hundreds of millions of pounds extra at the pumps.

The Government said on Tuesday night that officials were monitoring the situation, but the UK had a “diverse and resilient energy supply”.

Energy industry sources also cautioned that potential fuel shortages were an unlikely “worst-case scenario” that would only unfold if the US-Israeli war with Iran dragged on into the summer.

Markets have been sent into a panic by the conflict, driving up the price of oil and gas by 40pc and 60pc in the past four weeks.

The situation has been worsened by Iranian threats to strike ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz, effectively trapping one-fifth of the world’s oil and LNG supplies in The Gulf.

In Asia, that has triggered a scramble for alternatives – putting countries such as China, Japan and South Korea in competition with Europe for shipments from America, the world’s largest oil and LNG exporter.

Ashley Kelty, an oil and gas analyst at Panmure Liberum, said: “There are lots of American cargoes now being redirected to Asia because they are paying more.”

Households fear looming recession

A senior UK energy industry source acknowledged that the situation described by Shell’s chief executive was “one of the worst-case scenarios but still absolutely possible”.

The source added: “At the moment, people are less worried about the physical security of supply and more about prices.

“Remember, even at the height of the last energy crisis, when we lost huge gas volumes from Europe, we still managed to ensure security of supply.”

But they added: “Clearly there is a point – and it is hard to know exactly when, perhaps June or July – where you get substantial rises in prices and the question becomes whether Europe is actually willing to pay to get it.”

At that point, they said, prices may be so high that households and businesses start to self-ration.

Ministers have powers under the Energy Act to take control of petrol supplies during emergencies. Those powers were previously used in 2000 when striking hauliers blockaded fuel depots.

However, Adam Bell, a former government energy official at consultancy Stonehaven, said he did not think Britain was nearing that situation yet.

Instead, he expects ministers to reach for “softer” demand curbs such as driving speed restrictions or home-working guidance before contemplating any tougher measures.

He added: “If you are going to do anything in the short term, it will be that.”

To combat the energy squeeze, the European Commission has already urged member states to reduce gas storage targets for the coming winter.

Separately on Tuesday, Wall Street bankers said the UK was heading for a recession if the energy price shock continues.

Morgan Stanley said the Bank of England could raise interest rates if energy prices fail to come down, which would be likely to cause an economic downturn by the end of 2026.

Bruna Skarica, the chief UK economist at Morgan Stanley, said Britain would face “a pronounced UK recession at the turn of the year” if energy prices stay at recent highs in the coming months and borrowing costs rise.

Simon French, the chief economist at Panmure Liberum, said a recession in the final six months of the year was now “a real possibility”.

Huw Pill, the chief economist at the Bank of England, also warned that “there are limits to what the MPC [monetary policy committee] can do” to protect households from the energy price shock.

The fallout from the Middle East could also lead to food inflation hitting 8pc by June 2026 if the oil shock persists, according to the Institute of Grocery Distribution. That is more than double the current rate of 3.6pc.

Middle classes may be excluded from support

Rachel Reeves has suggested she will exclude the middle classes from any future support on energy bills.

The Chancellor told the Commons on Tuesday that contingency planning was under way to keep costs down as the conflict in the Middle East continued.

However, she said that she would not roll out an “unfunded, untargeted package of support” similar to the one introduced by the Tories after the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

Household energy bills were capped at £2,500 despite soaring oil and gas prices provoked by Moscow’s war with Kyiv.

Ms Reeves said: “As we respond to this crisis, we must learn from the mistakes of the past.

“The previous government pushed up borrowing, interest rates, inflation and mortgage costs with an unfunded, untargeted package of support under Liz Truss that gave support to the most wealthiest of households.”

Ms Reeves said energy bill support would be “for those who need it most”, adding that she would “update on fuel pricing within the next month”.

The support will reportedly be limited to people on benefits because officials will struggle to develop a system to means-test support for all poorer households in time.

Ms Reeves asked about the potential for establishing an “income threshold” for lower-earning households but has been told such a system will not be ready quickly enough, The Times reported.

HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) records the income of individuals, but energy bill support will have to be targeted at households. A project to link up the information began in January but is set to take more than a year.

The Chancellor is instead likely to rely on eligibility criteria for the Warm Homes Discount, which is limited to those on means-tested benefits.

data.title: Co-op boss steps down after staff criticise ‘toxic culture’
data.description:
The chief executive of the Co-op has resigned just weeks after she was accused of presiding over a “toxic culture” at the business.

Shirine Khoury-Haq has confirmed she will leave the retailer at the end of this week, bringing an end to a turbulent four years in charge, capped off by a major cyber attack last year.

The Co-op said on Thursday that it swung to a £126m loss in 2025 as a result of the breach, compared to a profit of £45m the year prior.

Ms Khoury-Haq said it had “been an honour” to lead the group, with her resignation confirmed just weeks after senior staff wrote a letter to the Co-op board complaining of “fear and alienation”.

Some employees said they felt unable to raise concerns about the direction of the business in front of the leadership team, including Ms Khoury-Haq, according to the letter seen by the BBC.

Lawyers for the Co-op told the BBC they did not recognise the critical comments raised, and “do not believe that they represent the views of our broader leadership and colleagues”.

Ms Khoury-Haq said: “Following last year’s cyber attack, the organisation is now ready to deliver on an ambitious strategy of stabilisation and transformation.

“This extends beyond the timeframe I had planned for my chief executive tenure, and now is the right moment to hand over to leadership that can commit to seeing the strategy through.”

During her tenure, the Beirut-born chief executive had driven a 95pc reduction in Co-op’s debt and a 30pc increase in profit between 2022 and 2024.

She also sought to champion the Co-op’s diversity and inclusion credentials, vowing last year to “protect trans people to the end” after the Supreme Court declared that transgender women were not legally women.

Ms Khoury-Haq wrote to its 54,000 staff in the wake of the ruling, saying she would “continue to advocate” for them and “speak out against hate”.

As well as announcing her departure, Co-op also said on Thursday that the cyber attack last April had delivered a £285m blow to revenues and knocked £107m from profits.

In its latest trading update, bosses said the business would be “recovering in 2026 with market share returning to or surpassing pre-attack levels in every business area”.

The Co-op runs more than 2,300 convenience stores across the UK, and more than 800 funeral parlours. It also runs insurance, legal and wholesale divisions

Chairman Debbie White said: “We thank Shirine for her leadership and for the significant contribution she has made to our Co-op, to our communities and to the co-operative movement during her tenure.

“The board is grateful for her commitment and leadership, particularly during a challenging few years, and we wish her every success in the future.”

Kate Allum, a member-nominated director on the co-operative’s board, will become interim chief executive from Monday while a search is carried out for a permanent successor.

She said it was a “privilege” to take the role “at such an important time for our Co-op”.

“Ours is a business with a clear purpose, a compelling business strategy and a values-led approach to making a positive difference within the communities we serve across the UK,” she said.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: £160m wiped off Currys as boss behind turnaround quits
data.description:
More than £160m was wiped off the value of Currys after the electrical goods giant announced the departure of its chief executive.

Alex Baldock will leave after eight years running the retailer, having spearheaded a successful turnaround and fended off two takeover attempts from foreign suitors.

Confirmation of his exit led to shares in Currys falling by more than 11pc on Thursday, the biggest drop in two years.

Before Thursday’s announcement, shares had risen by more than 160pc in the past three years, with the business now worth £1.33bn.

He has been credited with overseeing a revival in the company’s fortunes after it struggled for years in the wake of a 2014 merger with Carphone Warehouse.

Analysts at City broker Panmure Liberum said Mr Baldock had left Currys a “significantly stronger business than the one he inherited in 2018, when his turnaround strategy was first introduced”.

As well as navigating competition from Amazon and China’s e-commerce giants, he also successfully repelled takeover interest from Chinese online shopping empire JD.com and Wall Street activist investor Elliott in 2024.

Panmure Liberum said Currys had “moved from a net-debt to a net-cash position and restored margins back to pre-Covid levels in the face of tough macroeconomic conditions”.

Ian Dyson, the Currys chairman, said Mr Baldock had “achieved a huge amount” during his tenure, including “transforming the business in the face of some difficult headwinds”.

He added: “Currys is very well positioned for future success with a strategy that is clearly working, great financial health and a very strong leadership team.”

Adam Tomlinson, of German private bank Berenberg, said Mr Baldock had “led the most impressive UK retail turnaround of recent years”.

Mr Baldock is leaving to take an external position but will stay in post while the search for a successor is carried out.

He said: “My eight years at Currys have been simply the best of my career, and I’ll always be a loyal Currys customer, advocate and shareholder.

“Currys’ next chapter can be its most exciting yet. But it’s time for someone else to steer the business there, and time for me to move on to a new opportunity.”

As well as announcing Mr Baldock’s departure, Currys insisted it had traded in line with expectations since January, despite the global turmoil caused by the conflict in the Middle East.

It expects yearly profits to settle between £180m and £190m, an increase of up to 17pc on the previous year.

data.title: Disney abandons $1bn deal with OpenAI after it cancels ‘AI slop’ app
data.description:
Disney has cancelled a $1bn (£750m) investment in OpenAI after the tech giant killed its AI video app after just six months.

In an abrupt announcement, the company behind ChatGPT said it would shut down Sora, a service that allowed users to create hyper-realistic AI videos. The decision comes as OpenAI seeks to control its spiralling costs.

The move also sees the end of a $1bn licensing deal with Disney tied to the app, announced just three months ago.

“We’re saying goodbye to the Sora app,” OpenAI said. “To everyone who created with Sora, shared it, and built a community around it: thank you.”

A Disney spokesman said: “We respect OpenAI’s decision to exit the video generation business and to shift its priorities elsewhere.”

The launch of Sora last year was seen as a threat to the modern film industry and acting jobs. Its app also allowed users to use copyrighted characters from the likes of Disney and Nintendo in a casual way.

OpenAI eventually began blocking users from creating copyrighted characters following backlash from Hollywood. In December, it signed a $1bn investment deal with Disney, apparently drawing a line under the dispute.

Disney even said it would allow curated Sora clips on its Disney+ app.

But despite attracting more than 10 million downloads, Sora proved staggeringly expensive, requiring huge amounts of computing power to generate viral videos – dubbed by critics as “AI slop”.

According to one estimate in Forbes, it may have cost as much as $15m per day in computing power alone. Billy Peebles, the head of Sora, said last year: “The economics are currently completely unsustainable.”

It comes as Sam Altman, OpenAI’s chief executive, scrambles to put a lid on soaring costs as he seeks to list the company later this year at a valuation of up to $1tn.

OpenAI has been racing to shore up its finances after pledging to spend as much as $600bn on AI data centres by 2030.

Last month, it announced it had raised $100bn from investors, including Amazon and Nvidia. Yet it has projected it will lose $14bn this year alone.

Despite enjoying huge success with consumers through its ChatGPT app, Mr Altman has been seeking to trim costly side projects and focus on more lucrative business customers.

Anthropic, which develops the rival Claude AI app, has enjoyed glowing reviews from businesses. The rival AI lab has largely avoided launching flashy products aimed solely at consumers and eschewed video and image generation entirely.

data.title: Miliband urged to back Shetland gas fields that could fuel UK for five years
data.description:
Britain should move to tap five years’ worth of gas reserves lying near the Shetland Islands, a North Sea energy company has said.

Serica Energy says an estimated five trillion cubic feet of gas lies under the seabed west of Shetland, where there is already infrastructure for piping it back to the mainland.

The claim will pile fresh pressure on Ed Miliband to allow drilling in the North Sea. The Energy Secretary has faced mounting calls to rethink his position on oil and gas amid growing warnings of looming gas shortages linked to the Iran war.

Chris Cox, Serica’s chief executive, said: “Of course [five trillion cubic feet of gas], that sounds like a big number, and it is.

“In fact, it’s equivalent to supplying every household in the UK for five years. And yet, some people continue to say that the amount of gas we can produce in the UK is not significant.”

Serica, which published its 2025 results on Thursday, said its data showed there were many other potential new gas fields in the area.

It follows Wednesday’s Commons clash between Kemi Badenoch and Sir Keir Starmer over Labour’s blocking of two new North Sea oil and gas fields.

Mr Miliband has so far resisted granting approval for plans to exploit the Rosebank field west of Shetland, and Jackdaw in the North Sea. Both are in the final stages of development and could be pouring gas into UK pipes within months.

The fields cited by Serica lie in the same area west of Shetland as Rosebank, some with licences and some without. The latter would fall foul of Mr Miliband’s ban on new exploration and licencing.

Mr Cox said the gas deposits west of Shetland were potentially huge.

“We are very excited by the overall potential,” he said.

Serica has acquired some of the rights while others are held by rival companies such as Adura and Ithaca, whom Mr Cox described as equally “bullish” about the drilling prospects in the area.

Their key advantage is that they lie close enough to existing platforms and pipelines to be developed quickly, meaning they could be in operation within a couple of years. Fuel shortages and price rises caused by the Iran conflict are now predicted to last at least that long.

David Latin, Serica’s chairman, said the Iran conflict showed the need for a rethink by Labour. He called on Mr Miliband to accelerate approvals for licenced fields, lift the ban on new licences and scrap the windfall tax.

He said: “There is significant untapped oil and gas potential on the UK continental shelf … maximising the benefits available to the UK from domestic oil and gas, and achieving net zero by 2050 are not mutually exclusive objectives.

“Indeed, they complement each other, not least when oil and gas imported over thousands of miles typically comes with significantly higher emissions than the equivalent domestic production.”

A government spokesperson argued that the best way for the UK to protect itself was to reduce fossil fuel use, adding: “Issuing new licences to explore new fields cannot give us energy security and will not take a penny off bills.”

Mr Cox warned that the Labour government’s decision to extend the windfall tax by two years to 2030 had hit it hard – leaving the company with an effective overall tax rate of 165pc.

The extension of the windfall tax forced Serica to set aside $130m (£97m), and turned a $80m pre-tax profit into a post tax loss of $52m.

Serica was founded in 2004 and aimed at buying up older fields from larger companies. It is now one of the biggest oil and gas producers in the UK.

Last year its 11 fields produced the equivalent of 118m barrels of oil – half of which was gas that fed into UK pipes.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: British factories brace for an energy crisis worse than the 1970s
data.description:
Europe’s fuel supplies could be squeezed within days, and governments need to act.

That is the ominous warning from Wael Sawan, the boss of Shell boss, who this week said “a ripple effect” could see the continent’s energy security hit “as we get into April”.

A growing number of governments across the world are already introducing exceptional measures to limit the amount of fuel people use as the world reels from the Middle East energy supply shock triggered by war in Iran.

South Korea’s residents have been told to drive less, take shorter showers and use washing machines and vacuum cleaners only on the weekend.

In Sri Lanka, the government has declared that public institutions should take Wednesday off. The island nation has also implemented fuel rationing, with drivers now limited to buying 15 litres of petrol or diesel a day for private cars.

Thailand, with its tropical climate, has called on workers to swap suits for t-shirts in an effort to reduce the use of air conditioning. Swimming pools are no longer being heated in Mauritius and fountains have been turned off at the government’s direction.

All that may seem like a distant concern – something affecting far-off lands with vastly different economies and climates to ours.

Yet restrictions are already beginning to hit Europe. Slovenia became the first EU country this week to introduce fuel rationing, limiting drivers to 50 litres a day.

Here in the UK, ministers insist fuel supplies are secure and are urging people to go about their days as normal. Yet if this crisis drags on, that may not last.

The Telegraph has reported that strict speed limits could be imposed under the National Emergency Plan for Fuel, should ministers choose to activate it.

Adam Bell, a former government energy official at consultancy Stonehaven, said home-working guidance may also be likely if the Government decides to bring in measures to curb energy consumption in the coming months.

“If you are going to do anything in the short term, it will be that,” he says.

“If you do not have enough fuel, governments have to think very carefully about how they use the fuel they have, what is still coming in, and what they hold in inventories,” says Callum MacPherson, the head of commodities at Investec.

Ashley Kelty, of investment bank Panmure Liberum, said limits on the amount of gas factories can use may also be considered.

Even if the Government doesn’t step in, manufacturers may adopt self-imposed limits as costs skyrocket.

The average annual electricity bill for small industrial and commercial businesses has risen by 20pc, or £96,000, to £578,000 since the war in Iran began, figures from Cornwall Insight show.

For those businesses that use gas – including chemical producers, glass and ceramics factories and metalworks – the pain is even worse. Average annual gas bills have risen by nearly 60pc to just more than £1m.

“Many of these companies are already battling slimmer margins, so this rise in energy costs is not something they can simply absorb,” Jacob Briggs, from Cornwall Insight, says.

“For some firms, this could mean the difference between investing in growth this year or shelving their plans entirely, and for others, high bills could force some very difficult economic decisions.”

Arjan Geveke, the director of the Energy Intensive Users Group, told The Telegraph last week there was a “high risk” that factories would start voluntarily cutting production in the coming weeks because of high energy costs.

Kelty fears that the impact of the crisis on the UK economy could be “horrendous”, with the automotive and steel manufacturing sectors the worst affected.

He said: “Even if the rockets stop tomorrow, the problem is that it’s going to take a month to get back to any form of normality in terms of restarting oil production and getting all the tankers out of the Gulf and to their original destinations.”

The UK will face a “very challenging” winter, as even if the conflict comes to an end, it will struggle to refill its gas storage, Kelty added.

All this is set to weigh on the economy. If the energy crisis continues and Brent crude oil remains at around $100 a barrel, Capital Economics has forecast that the UK will enter a mild recession by the end of the year.

Jennifer McKeown, chief global economist from Capital Economics, said: “The energy-intensive sectors of industry, hotels and retail are likely to be hit the hardest.

“The sharp increase in the cost of energy is going to mean that it is quite likely that industrial production will need to be cut back.”

In the summer of 2022, as energy prices soared following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, many European nations imposed policies to curb oil and gas consumption.

Measures at the time included restrictions on air conditioning and reduced lighting for national monuments and advertising boards.

By late 2022, Britons were encouraged to cut energy use during peak times, with some electricity providers offering reduced costs in an effort to encourage households to shift consumption to off-peak hours.

Restrictions were far more draconian during the Arab oil crisis of the 1970s. In 1973, drivers were using fuel ration books and were advised to reduce their maximum speed to 50mph.

By early 1974, fuel shortages and rationing meant that many industrial businesses were operating with a three-day working week and TV channels were shut off after 10.30pm.

Fatih Birol, executive director of the International Energy Agency (IEA), has said that the current energy supply shock is worse than the 1970s. “No country will be immune to the effects of this crisis if it continues to go in this direction,” he has warned. “So there is a need for global efforts.”

The IEA has called for car-free Sundays globally and urged people to work from home three days a week in order to conserve fuel.

Ministers in Britain are understood to have found this intervention unhelpful. With the Government convinced the UK has enough fuel for the moment, their biggest concern is to avoid panic buying or unnecessary changes in business patterns that could create fresh problems.

However, disruption to energy supplies looks likely to continue. QatarEnergy, which operates the world’s largest liquefied natural gas facility, said on Tuesday that it was preparing to declare “force ‌majeure” on some fuel contracts.

The crisis measure would allow it to stop shipments agreed under contract without facing any legal liability. Italy, Belgium, South Korea ⁠and China will be affected.

MacPherson said: “If this went on and on, and we had to live in a world where there is much less oil than we have been used to in the past, people would have to travel less, and when they do travel, it would be more expensive.”

While Britain may have escaped the worst of it so far, the threat of an energy-triggered economic crunch looms.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Heathrow’s new car parks to cost more than Gatwick’s second runway
data.description:
From HS2 to Crossrail, British infrastructure projects lead the world in one unenviable respect: their sizeable, often runaway budgets.

The bill for big-ticket construction schemes typically starts off with eye-watering sums – and then climb to even more staggering levels as budgets are blown and costs run rampant.

Now a plan to provide 36,000 parking spaces at Heathrow airport is poised to join the pantheon of cash-hungry mega-projects after it was revealed that it will come with a staggering £2.6bn price tag.

That’s £400m more than the estimated outlay for a new runway at Gatwick and more than half the cost of Heathrow’s Terminal 5 – the largest free-standing structure in the UK.

At £72,000 per parking space, the two multi-storey car parks planned under the scheme will also be comfortably the most expensive in Britain and a contender for the costliest ever built.

Heathrow argues the price per space will be lower because only half the overall cost, about £1.3bn, is for the buildings themselves, with the rest of the spending going towards associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Yet the airport has faced criticism from some of the world’s largest airlines, who will ultimately pick up the bill for the world’s most expensive car park.

In a week when National Car Parks (NCP), the country’s biggest car park operator, collapsed into administration, the plan might seem an ambitious one.

However, unlike NCP, which was hit by a drop in commuting, Heathrow can rely on a captive customer base and funding rules that mean construction costs are ultimately likely to be passed on as aircraft landing fees.

Airline bosses have attacked the project as typical of Heathrow’s largesse in lavishing cash on gold-plated enhancements that they and their customers must ultimately pay for.

The airport says the car parks will be a vital component of its £49bn plan for a third runway that will lift passenger numbers from 84 million a year to as many as 150 million.

It says the sites – one next to the M4 and the other to the south near the M25 – will be needed to cope with the thousands of additional passengers driving to what is already Europe’s busiest airport each day, even if a higher proportion of people take public transport.

And it argues that the expense of the project reflects construction costs in London that are themselves amongst the highest in the world.

But Luis Gallego, the boss of British Airways (BA) owner International Airlines Group, said the plan was overpriced.

He highlighted the shortcomings of a funding model that allows Heathrow to charge airlines a regulated fee to cover building and operating costs.

“We support expansion, but not at any cost. All future spending must be carefully controlled as Heathrow already charges passengers twice as much as Amsterdam and three times as much as Paris,” he said.

Willie Walsh, the director general of the International Air Transport Association, was blunter, describing the car parks plan as “madness” and saying his mind “boggled” at the £2.6bn bill.

He said Heathrow had proposed what would have been the world’s most expensive car park when he was chief executive of BA as part of a previous runway plan that was rejected in 2010.

He said: “You can find data on what it costs to build a car park, there’s lots of evidence there, and Heathrow is just way out of line with the rest of the world.

“The problem is that they are incentivised to spend as much money as possible and to build the most expensive car park and to be proud of that.”

Heathrow has said that calling the new structures car parks does not do them justice.

Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, has instead referred to them as “parking villages”.

The blueprint for the new runway proposes “two large parkway car parks” that would replace more than 20 existing facilities, with the northern site able to accommodate 20,000 vehicles and the southern one 16,000.

It adds: “This could support fast and efficient connections to each terminal campus via dedicated transport systems.

“These new parkways could also provide local communities with improved access to airport terminals and public transport links and help reduce congestion on local roads.”

While a leaked Heathrow briefing paper states the cost of the parkways to be £2.6bn, a spokesman said the parking areas alone will consume only about half the budget, with the rest covering the cost of associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Major profit centre

The car parks may be necessary to cope with an influx of new passengers, but they would also be a major profit centre, with parking fees contributing £200m in revenue last year.

Heathrow’s long-stay car parks currently charge £46.80 for the first day and £37.40 for each additional 24-hour period.

Taking an average of £40 per day, the number of spaces planned could generate more than £500m in revenue over the course of a year.

That is not including income from drop-off charges and the short-stay car parks nearer to terminals that the airport has indicated it plans to retain.

Michael O’Leary, the chief executive of Ryanair, which does not fly from Heathrow, said drop-off fees were “a scandal” across UK airports and that parking had been turned into a cash cow integral to their business models.

He said: “The more they earn from car parks the more they will dividend it out to their shareholders and then complain that they can’t make a reasonable return on the risks they were taking investing in runways in London.

“But there’s no risk at all, it’s a complete freebie, which is why pension funds now own most of these airports.”

O’Leary said the cost of runways, car parks and terminals was ultimately the result of a “broken” regulatory model that “rewards inefficiency and incentivises airports to squander gazillions”.

A study from Oxford Global Projects commissioned by Heathrow Reimagined, a lobby group formed by airlines including BA, has suggested that Heathrow’s construction costs across a variety of infrastructure including car parks are consistently among the highest in the world.

The report said the cost of the runway alone – covering cement, asphalt and lights – would come in at more than £800,000 per metre, making it one of the most expensive ever built.

Jonathan Simcock, author of The Delivery Gap, an examination of failed infrastructure projects, said companies like Heathrow have an incentive to inflate estimated costs by “including all the bells and whistles they can”.

In so doing, he said, they aim to secure regulatory approval for levels of spending – in Heathrow’s case to be funded by airlines and their customers – that will cover all eventualities and “ensure they are not left holding the baby”.

“If the estimate is inflated then the company ends up making an inappropriate profit. But if it’s undercooked the company gets into trouble, somebody has to bail them out and the country doesn’t get the infrastructure it needs,” he says.

Woldbye has said that the costings for the car parks and the wider runway project could change and that Heathrow is open to conversations about modifying its plans.

However, Simcock warned: “When the conversation is about how it can possibly cost that much, very often it turns out that it should have been, can you really do it for that?

“For very large and highly complex HS2-type things – and Heathrow is big and complex – it is rare to get nice surprises and much more common to get nasty ones.”

data.title: Energy bills bailout ‘could pay for itself’, says City
data.description:
An energy bailout for millions of households would pay for itself, economists have said.

Deutsche Bank said a support package worth £4bn would ease inflation by holding down gas and electricity bills.

Economists said this would result in lower interest rates, which in turn would save the Government billions of pounds in reduced borrowing costs.

The intervention is likely to raise pressure on Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, after she has so far resisted calls to roll out a wide-ranging energy-bills support package after criticising the cost of the Tories’ bailout in the wake of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

However, Deutsche Bank’s Sanjay Raja said any potential bailout could “pay for itself”.

“Fiscal support that would actively reduce CPI inflation could end up paying for itself – even adding to the fiscal headroom,” he said.

It comes as EDF and E.On Next, two of Britain’s biggest energy suppliers, warned that household gas and electricity bills could top £2,000 by autumn because of the war in Iran.

EDF suggests annualised bills will rise to £2,047 by October, up from its forecasts of £1,937 for July. E.On Next has similar figures, predicting £1,955 from July and £2,064 from October.

EDF also warned that prices could rise even further by October.

It said: “Our forecasts from July 2026 onwards have continued to rise week-on-week, driven by increases in wholesale prices arising from ongoing strikes in Iran and the Middle East.”

EDF and Eon collectively supply around seven million households in the UK.

The expected increase in bills is a direct consequence of the conflict in the Middle East, which has sent oil and gas prices soaring.

As well as higher energy bills, households have also been hit by soaring petrol and diesel prices.

Petrol now costs more than £1.50 per litre, up from below £1.33 at the end of February, according to the RAC. Diesel is up more than 35p at nearly £1.78 on average.

This has prompted calls for Ms Reeves to postpone the rise in fuel duty scheduled to take effect in September.

If the Chancellor freezes fuel duty and cuts £150 from household bills, this would ultimately cost the Government £4bn, said Mr Raja.

But it would also trim 0.4 percentage points off inflation. His calculations indicate this could be enough to take around 0.3 percentage points off interest rates in financial markets.

Combined with the boost to growth that would come from avoiding the blow from higher rates, Mr Raja suggests that the Government would have to borrow £5bn less than expected.

“In effect, paying for the entirety of fiscal support levied to reduce consumer price inflation,” he said.

The idea of government support lowering borrowing costs has also been raised by Zack Polanski.

The Green Party leader said this month that “the Government should guarantee right now that it will not allow energy bills to rise beyond the April-June price cap”, proposing an £8.4bn scheme.

He said: “If the price cap rises, we will see interest rates rise, mortgage rates up, bond yields up and inflation up.

“And we will be back into the doom loop that has done untold damage to our economy and caused misery for households across the UK for years now.”

Britain is particularly exposed to inflation from global markets because it is a net importer of energy.

Economists expect Britain to face a jump in inflation, which is potentially twice as big as that facing the eurozone.

A survey by the National Institute of Economic and Social Research found the average economist expected an increase in inflation of 0.8 to 1.1 percentage points in the UK, compared with 0.4 to 0.7 in the eurozone.

data.title: Housebuilder facing cash squeeze handed £300m by government
data.description:
Labour is under pressure to explain why the Government has handed a troubled housebuilder more than £300m in taxpayer-backed loans and contracts.

Paul Holmes, the shadow housing minister, said there were “questions about Labour’s judgement” after it partnered with Vistry to deliver affordable housing.

The London-listed building firm has been handed around £315m in grants and loans, according to Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister.

Homes England, the housing quango responsible for allocating the funds, does not directly monitor the financial viability of the companies it works with, he added.

Shares in Vistry have crashed by more than 50pc over the past month following the surprise departure of Greg Fitzgerald, chief executive, and amid concerns about a cash squeeze at the business.

Vistry has announced plans to offer deep discounts on some of its homes to offload them and raise capital. Reports suggest it has also demanded delays on paying subcontractors and asked for discounts as it battles to preserve cash.

It follows a string of profit warnings after bosses underestimated the cost of building homes in the south of England.

Paul Holmes, the Conservative shadow Housing minister, submitted an official parliamentary question to Mr Pennycook last week on the taxpayer’s “financial commitment to Vistry”.

“Vistry’s plight raises serious doubts about Labour’s ability to deliver their so-called Affordable Homes Programme,” Mr Holmes told The Telegraph.

“And it also raises questions about Labour’s judgement. If the Government isn’t properly scrutinising the companies it partners with, how can it say whether they will succeed or fail?

“Labour must come clean about how and why ministers concluded Vistry was the best choice – and tell us how much taxpayers’ money could be lost.”

In November, ministers cleared Vistry to bid for as much as £700m in taxpayer subsidies as part of the Government’s £39bn affordable homes programme. It has the same status under the existing programme, begun by the Conservatives in 2021.

Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets said there were “amber flags” about Vistry’s financial position.

“Vistry needs to slow down, shrink the business, stabilise the cash situation before it sees growth,” he said.

Mr Codling added that the going concern note in Vistry’s latest financial accounts had grown from around 350 words to more than 1,700 words, three times the size that it was this time last year, which “paints a darker downside” than before.

The wider economic backdrop also offers little comfort, with housebuilders warning of inflationary uncertainty stemming from the conflict in Iran.

“If the market really turns down, it’ll get a lot trickier for Vistry,” said Aynsley Lammin of Investec.

Mr Pennycook said that the Affordable Homes Programme does not carry hard credit risk exposure on companies it works with, as it is a grant programme, so a “purposely high-level review” is undertaken instead.

The ministers added that there are “ no checks undertaken on the off-balance sheet liabilities” as it “is not the role of Homes England to undertake liquidity tests on strategic partners”.

A Vistry spokesman said that taxpayer money was not at risk and said it “has been deployed on building affordable homes that this country desperately needs”.

They added that Vistry “continues to maintain a comfortable level of headroom against its banking facilities”.

“We work closely with Homes England on the delivery of new homes, and, like all of its partners, our programme is closely monitored and audited,” they said.

“The grant funding has all been committed, and the homes for which the funds were allocated are now contracted and owned by regulated, registered providers.”

In March, Mr Fitzgerald vowed to shrink Vistry’s debt by the end of this year.

A spokesman for Homes England said: “Homes England operates within robust, transparent frameworks when working with delivery partners. We don’t comment on the commercial or financial position of individual partners.”

The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The green-belt village about to fall under the shadow of Europe’s largest data centre
data.description:
“The peace, quiet and openness of the country.” That’s why Danny Leach and his family first moved to North Ockendon, on the north-east side of Greater London, back in 2005.

Despite being barely outside the M25 and a part of the London Borough of Havering, the village and surrounding countryside are remarkably quiet.

The fenland surrounding the village is undeveloped and, considering its proximity to the capital, surprisingly biodiverse.

“I’ve got nearly 100 species of birds that visit my garden, including kingfishers, barn owls and tawny owls,” says Leach. “There are also great crested newts and foxes. On a summer evening, I can hear the frogs croaking.”

Part of the reason such an environment can exist so close to a city of 10 million is the Metropolitan Green Belt, which, since its creation in 1938, has protected the countryside near London and prevented urban sprawl.

Now, a proposed Green Belt-busting development could change the area’s character forever.

Havering council recently launched a consultation on “East Havering Data Centre”, a hyperscale AI facility that would make North Ockendon home to the largest data centre in Europe.

The facility would use 540 acres of existing farmland and require 600 megawatts of electricity to operate – the same amount of energy needed to run one million homes.

Developer Digital Reef says the site will include around 84 acres of data centre buildings, along with a 280-acre ecological park.

Residents were outraged to learn that the consultation period for the 2,700-page proposal would last only 30 days, clashing with the May elections, and that Havering council were processing the project via a “Local Development Order”, a special type of planning tool that allows conventional planning permission steps to be skipped.

Labour and Conservative members of the council’s strategic planning committee advocated a longer, 12-week consultation period, but were outvoted by the Havering Residents Association, a local party that runs the council.

Data centres are essentially large warehouses filled with specialist computing equipment that processes AI requests submitted by users on their phones or computers. East Havering Data Centre is one of hundreds of these projects proposed near Britain’s major cities to power the growing demand for AI, many of which will be built on green-belt land.

Data centres require large amounts of energy and water to operate, raising concerns that construction plans could put pressure on electricity and water infrastructure and drive up household bills.

Leach is one of many North Ockendon residents who are unhappy with the proposal. His property, which backs on to the site, will be particularly affected.

‘People move to the country for a reason’

“It’s a big shock,” he says. “People move to the countryside for a reason, and then suddenly you have something the size of a battleship going up at the end of your garden.”

Local businesses are also worried. Daniel Anthony has run Top Meadow Golf Club his entire working life, taking over from his father.

Since launching a nine-hole golf club in 1985, the family has grown the business into an 18-hole club and 20-room hotel that employs 50 people.

“Everything, everything, everything has been reinvested from day one,” says Anthony. He is worried the project is putting a lifetime of work at risk.

Today, Anthony says the club makes most of its money from functions hosted at the hotel and restaurant: “We do anything from birthdays and anniversaries to funerals, which are a big one for us because it’s only two and a half miles to the nearest crematorium.”

East Havering Data Centre will be built directly across the road from Top Meadow. Anthony is worried that the facility will be a blight on the club’s prized views of the countryside. “It’s the view really; that’s what people come for,” he says.

He is also concerned about industrial traffic and possible traffic diversions during the construction. A traffic diversion could extend the journey from Top Meadow to the nearby crematorium from two and a half to 10 miles, threatening the club’s funeral and wake business.

The rise of data centres

Developer Digital Reef estimates the facility will take four years to construct – longer than most data centre projects.

Ed Griffiths, of construction consultancy Barbour ABI, says construction is relatively fast for most data centres, with build programmes averaging 19 to 30 months, depending on scale and power requirements.

Sites near large cities are ideal for AI data centres, as user requests can be processed more quickly.

“Urban fringe locations are common for data centres, particularly where they provide proximity to users while still offering enough space and infrastructure to support large facilities,” says Griffiths.

This makes the green-belt land that surrounds many of Britain’s cities tempting for developers.

Data provided to The Telegraph by Barbour ABI indicate that 13 of the 100 largest data centres planned in the UK would fall within the green belt.

These green-belt data centres have a combined value of just over £10bn, representing an outsized share of the 100 largest planned projects at 19pc of the £52bn total.

Data centre construction is a priority for the Government, which views the facilities as essential for turning Britain into an “AI superpower” and for growing the economy.

In a January 2025 speech, Sir Keir Starmer said AI will be “mainlined into the veins of the nation” and has classed data centres as “critical national infrastructure”.

‘Destroying a rich ecosystem’

Many developers include environmental concessions, such as the East Havering Data Centre’s “ecology park” in London, in their planning applications as a sweetener for nearby communities.

However, Ian Pirie, a Havering Friends of the Earth co-ordinator, is sceptical of their benefits.

“Developers think that they can just tear up all that land and then dig somewhere else, plant some trees, and they’ve restored the ecosystem. It’s complete nonsense,” says Pirie.

“They are destroying an already existing, rich ecosystem. It would take another 100 years to have what we’ve got there now.”

Another local concern is the data centre’s potential water usage. The facilities often use water cooling to prevent computers from overheating.

Digital Reef has proposed that excess heat generated by the data centre will be redirected to heat local homes and a novel 12-acre indoor avocado farm.

But residents are unconvinced, telling The Telegraph that the local water supply is already temperamental and that industrial usage could push it to breaking point.

A spokesman for North Ockendon Residents’ Association says: “The water supply here is very old, under-maintained pipe work. If they were to then start taking thousands of gallons a day and something happened, we would be without water.”

Griffiths, who works at Barbour ABI, says access to grid power is the most important factor in selecting a site for a data centre, followed by water availability.

Data centres’ use of energy and water resources could have a national impact. Ireland, which is a hub for trans-Atlantic data cables and hosts the European headquarters of American tech giants such as Amazon and Google, has prioritised its data centre infrastructure.

In 2024, Ireland’s data centres used 22pc of its total electricity supply, more than the 18pc used by the country’s urban homes, according to the Central Statistics Office. There are concerns that a similar build-out in the UK would drive up energy costs, squeezing consumers.

A spokesman for Havering Local Planning Authority says that a full evidence base for East Havering Data Centre would be considered as part of the application, including assessments on biodiversity, ecology, economic impact, transport, water usage and use of the green belt. 

Digital Reef could not be reached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Virgin Media O2 threatens to stop building broadband if £2bn deal blocked
data.description:
The co-owner of Virgin Media O2 (VMO2) has threatened to stop building broadband in the UK if the telecoms giant’s £2bn takeover of Netomnia is blocked.

Mike Fries, the chief executive of Liberty Global, which owns VMO2 alongside Telefónica, warned he could pull the plug on investment if regulators give in to criticism by blocking the deal to buy their rival.

The complex transaction, agreed last month, will see Liberty Global and Telefónica team up with InfraVia, a French private equity firm, to buy Netomnia through their joint venture Nexfibre.

The tie-up will bolster VMO2’s position as the main challenger to incumbent BT, creating a combined full-fibre network reaching roughly eight million homes.

But the deal is facing fierce opposition from CityFibre, the UK’s third-largest broadband provider, which has urged regulators to intervene based on the claim that it will “significantly reduce competition”.

Speaking at a New Street Research conference in London on Tuesday, Mr Fries said: “There’s no theory of harm here that stacks up, and I can tell you all the counterfactuals are bad.

“Maybe we stop building fibre, or Nexfibre stops building fibre ... the only counterfactual that’s certain is BT gets stronger.”

VMO2’s takeover of Netomnia has been viewed by some industry observers as the trigger for a wave of consolidation across Britain’s turbulent broadband market.

A wave of smaller firms, known as “altnets”, has cropped up to challenge BT’s dominance. However, rising interest rates and weaker-than-expected uptake have left many struggling to survive.

London-focused G Network collapsed into administration in January after being bought by vulture capital firm FitzWalter, which will now seek a buyer after stripping the company of its debts.

Mr Fries called complaints about the Netomnia deal “sour grapes”, adding: “CityFibre was trying to do the same deal so I don’t see how it’s a different outcome for Nexfibre versus CityFibre.”

CityFibre has previously called for consolidation in the altnet market, and was also exploring a potential bid for Netomnia.

However, it is thought that the debt-laden company, which is backed by Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund Mubadala and Goldman Sachs, would need to raise more money to finance any deals.

Speaking at the same event, Nick Dunn, the CityFibre finance chief, insisted that his company would have other takeover opportunities if VMO2’s Netomnia swoop goes ahead.

The Netomnia deal is widely expected to face an initial phase-one investigation by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA). It could also be referred to a more in-depth review.

Mr Fries urged regulators to approve the deal after phase one, arguing that blocking the transaction would undermine Labour’s pledge to kickstart economic growth.

The chief executive said: “The Government has talked about transforming regulation in this country, working at pace, not stifling innovation and growth. This is a test case for that.”

He added: “Who is going to build a scale-based competitor to BT Openreach? Who has the capital, the wherewithal, the customers? I don’t think there’s anybody else. The Government should see this as a net positive for sure.”

A CityFibre spokesman said: “For 20 years, VMO2 has failed to challenge BT’s dominance of the market. It’s only now that competition is emerging to both of those players, for the benefit of consumers and the UK. 

“The proposed deal would reduce choice for millions of homes and weaken the competitive pressure that has finally begun to transform the market. We have no doubt the CMA will do a thorough job of investigating the transaction.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.
